coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots promoted the construction of Z-scheme system with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xingzhong
Zhang, Jin
Yan, Ming
Si, Mengying
Jiang, Longbo
Li, Yifu
Yu, Hanbo
Zhang, Jiong
Zeng, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) as a visible-light photocatalyst is always restricted by rapid photoexcited electron-hole recombination. Hence, the construction of charge transfer path is an essential strategy to establish an efficient photocatalytic system. Notably, the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) exhibit potential for application in photocatalysis due to its high charge transfer capability and molecular oxygen activation ability. In this work, a Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O system with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability was constructed. Under the optimum condition, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite showed 99.1% tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal efficiency under visible light irradiation in 60 min. The N-CQDs provide a potential driving force to reestablish the local electric field and lead to the construction of the Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O system. And the excellent photocatalytic performance of this system was ascribed to the greatly enhanced molecular oxygen activation by contacting N-CQDs and the construction of Z-scheme system. Furthermore, the visible light absorption capability was obviously improved due to the fact that Cu2O provided multiple reflection of visible light and the N-CQDs act as a photosensitizer. Noteworthily, this study may help to further understand the function of N-CQDs in heterojunction and it could be expanded to design other photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
N-CQDs
Z scheme
Visible light photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O and Cu2O/BiVO4 composites
Morphology and textural property of photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites.
The XPS spectra of BiVO4/N-CQDs /Cu2O composite: (a) survey spectrum, (b–g) high-resolution spectra of Bi 4f, V 2p, O 1s, Cu 2p, N 1s and C 1s, respectively.
SEM images of (a, e) BiVO4, (b, f) Cu2O, (c, g) BiVO4/Cu2O and (d, h) BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite.

TEM images and element distribution images of the photocatalysts. (a) BiVO4, (b) Cu2O, (c-d) BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite and (e) N-CQDs. (f) HRTEM image of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite. (g–h) HAADF-STEM images of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O. (i–n) The corresponding EDS mapping images of Bi, V, Cu, O, N and C elements in the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite.
Photoluminescence spectra of the BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) the relative band gap energy of the photocatalysts.

(a) Transient photocurrent response of pure BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites under visible light irradiation. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of pure BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites.

Photocatalytic degradation of TC based on (a) BiVO4/N-CQDs composites with different content of N-CQDs and (b) BiVO4/N-CQDs-1/Cu2O composites with different Cu2O mass ratios under visible light irradiation. (c and d) Summary of the photocatalytic degradation of TC based on different photocatalysts.
(a) Recycle experiments for the photodegradation of TC by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite; (b) XRD patterns of the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite before and after the cycling experiments.
Trapping experiments of active species for photodegradation of TC under visible light irradiation.

ESR spectra of DMPO trapped (a)
O2 − and (b)
OH with BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite in the dark and under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm).
The possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite for the photodegradation of TC under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots promoted the construction of Z-scheme system with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Environment, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) as a visible-light photocatalyst is always restricted by rapid photoexcited electron-hole recombination. Hence, the construction of charge transfer path is an essential strategy to establish an efficient photocatalytic system. Notably, the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) exhibit potential for application in photocatalysis due to its high charge transfer capability and molecular oxygen activation ability. In this work, a Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O system with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability was constructed. Under the optimum condition, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite showed 99.1% tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal efficiency under visible light irradiation in 60 min. The N-CQDs provide a potential driving force to reestablish the local electric field and lead to the construction of the Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O system. And the excellent photocatalytic performance of this system was ascribed to the greatly enhanced molecular oxygen activation by contacting N-CQDs and the construction of Z-scheme system. Furthermore, the visible light absorption capability was obviously improved due to the fact that Cu2O provided multiple reflection of visible light and the N-CQDs act as a photosensitizer. Noteworthily, this study may help to further understand the function of N-CQDs in heterojunction and it could be expanded to design other photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics in the aqueous environment, mainly come from the discharge of industrial sewage, hospital, pharmaceutical and aquaculture wastewater. In addition, antibiotics continuous accumulating in the aqueous environment which would pose a serious threat to human health. Therefore, the elimination of antibiotics in water has become an urgent issue [1–3].

Semiconductor-based photocatalysis inspires the potential application in energy shortages and environmental crisis due to it holds the properties of low cost, high and high energy conservation efficiency [4–6]. Nowadays, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) as the photocatalyst for antibiotics photodegradation has drawn growing interest owing to its appropriate band gap energy (about 2.4 eV), relatively high physical-chemical stability and nontoxicity. Unfortunately, some intrinsic defects limit its application in photocatalysis, such as the low photoexcited charge separation efficiency and low visible light absorption efficiency (45% in the solar energy). The heterojunction construction is an effective strategy to ameliorate the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4. As previous reports, many semiconductors modified BiVO4 to form heterojunction. For instance, Bi2WO6/BiVO4 [7], WO3/BiVO4 [8], MoS2/BiVO4 [9] and so on. Nowadays, cuprous oxide (Cu2O) is regarded as a rising star due to its superior properties, such as narrow band gap energy (2.0–2.2 eV) and strong visible light absorption capability. Generally, the p-type Cu2O and n-type BiVO4 usually constructed p-n heterojunction [10]. And the improved photocatalytic performance obtained in this system due to the enhanced charge separation and light absorption. However, the traditional p-n heterojunction leads to relative low redox ability during the transferred process of electrons and holes [11]. Thus, it is significant to construct a new photocatalytic reaction system that could promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, enhance light absorption capability and maximize the redox ability simultaneously.

Among the numerous photocatalytic reaction system, the Z-scheme photocatalytic system not only increase the separation rate of electron-hole pairs but also maximize the redox potential of the heterojunction, which is favourable to the photocatalytic reaction process. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with small dimensions (<10 nm) have heralded a new chapter in photocatalysis due to its good electron capture capability and outstanding photochemistry properties [12–14]. Furthermore, π structure of CQDs can promote to construct a stable structure with other semiconductors [13,15], such as CQDs/Bi2WO6 [12], CQDs/Bi2SiO5 [16], CQDs/BiOX (X = Br, I) [17] and CQDs/WO3 [18]. Thus, the CQDs is expected to play as an electron mediator and promote the construction of Z-scheme photocatalytic system. However, the CQDs is still to be modified to pursue desirable physical and chemical properties. Nowadays, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been synthesized and show obvious advantages over CQDs. For instance, effectively induce charge delocalization, obviously improve the light harvesting and abundant of surface defects existed on the surface [15,19].

In this research, a novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) promoted Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability was successfully synthesized. The N-CQDs provide the potential driving force to reestablish local electric field and lead to the construction of Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O system. And the excellent photocatalytic performance of this system was ascribed to the greatly enhanced molecular oxygen activation by contacting N-CQDs and the construction of Z-scheme system. Furthermore, the visible light absorption capability was obviously improved due to the fact that Cu2O provided multiple reflection of visible light and the N-CQDs paly as a photosensitizer. The results demonstrated that a Z-scheme BiVO4/Cu2O system was constructed, resulting in outstanding electron capture capability, enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability and enhanced visible light harvesting ability.

BiVO4 and N-CQDs were fabricated via a hydrothermal method from the reported literatures, respectively [20,21]. N-CQDs/BiVO4 composite was prepared by the same method described in our previous report [22]. And the volume of N-CQDs was 1, 3, 5 and 7 mL, respectively. The as-prepared samples were labeled as BiVO4/N-CQDs-1, BiVO4/N-CQDs-3, BiVO4/N-CQDs-5, BiVO4/N-CQDs-7, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O and Cu2O/BiVO4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was prepared by a precipitation method: Briefly, a certain amount of N-CQDs/BiVO4 was added into 25 mL aqueous solution of Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O (0.05 M) and then the resulting mixture was sonicated for 30 min. After that, 3.5 mL NaOH solution (1 M) and 15 mL glucose solution (1 M) were dropwise added into the above mixture with continual magnetic stirring, respectively. The solution became gradually brick-red suspensions after stirring for 2 h at 40 °C. Afterward, the resulting precipitates were collected, rinsed with deionized water and ethanol repeatedly, then dried at 60 °C for 8 h. Finally, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was achieved. The nominal weight ratios of Cu2O to N-CQDs/BiVO4 were 5, 10, 15 and 20 wt%, and the obtained BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites were named as BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O −5, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O −10, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O-15 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O-20, respectively. By contrast, Cu2O/BiVO4 was also synthetized under the same condition by adding the BiVO4 take the place of N-CQDs/BiVO4.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on Bruker D8 Advance with Cu Kα radiation. The chemical composition and chemical states of the samples were studied by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) on ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were recorded by FEI-quanta 200F scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and high angle angular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images were acquired using F20 S-TWIN TMP electron microscope (Tecnai G2, FEI Co.) operated at 200 kV. The UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded on U4100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on Edinburgh FLsp 920 transient fluorescence spectrometer. The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments were performed using an electrochemical station (CHI660D, Shanghai Chenhua Ltd., China) in a conventional three-electrode configuration. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped by reagent DMPO were obtained on a Bruker model ESR E500 spectrometer.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated by the photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) under visible light (λ > 420 nm). A 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing CEL Tech. Co., Ltd.) was used as the source with a 420 nm cutoff filter to provide visible-light irradiation. The photocatalytic experiments were carried out as follows: 30 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of TC (10 mg/L) aqueous solution. Before illumination, the suspension was constantly stirred for 30 min in dark to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During the illumination a 4 mL solution was collected at every 10 min intervals, and the aqueous solution centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the photocatalysts. Finally, the concentration of TC was analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–2550, SHIMADZU, Japan) at the characteristic peak of 357 nm for TC.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites are shown in Fig. 1 . The position and relative intensity of all diffraction peaks can be indexed to monoclinic BiVO4 phase, compared with JCPDS NO. 14-0688 [23]. The XRD pattern of N-CQDs/BiVO4 is similar with that of pure BiVO4 due to the low amount and good dispersion of N-CQDs [19]. For the XRD patterns of Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites, a weak peak was appeared at 36.4° which corresponds to the (1 1 1) plane of Cu2O [10]. Meanwhile, no impurity was detected in all the patterns, indicating that the high purity of the as-prepared samples.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further investigate the elemental compositions and chemical states of the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite. As presented in Fig. 2 a, the XPS survey spectrum of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite shows the Bi, V, O, Cu, N and C are the main elements. Fig. 2b shows the high resolution spectrum of Bi 4f, two strong peaks located at 163.8 eV and 158.5 eV corresponding to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 [24]. Fig. 2c is the V 2p spectrum, the core lines fixed at 523.6 eV and 516.1 eV could be assigned to V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2, respectively [25]. The O 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d, two dominant peaks can be divided into three peaks located at 531.1 eV, 529.6 eV and 529.1 eV, which were assigned to CO/CO, OH− on the surface of the as-prepared samples and BiO bonds of (Bi2O2)2+ in BiVO4, respectively [19,26,27]. Fig. 2e presents Cu 2p spectrum, two characteristic peaks located at 952.5 eV and 932.6 eV belonged to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 binding energies of Cu2O crystallites, respectively [28]. The N 1s peak located at 401.1 eV was come from the NH in the N-CQDs(Fig. 2f) [21,29]. And the C 1s XPS spectra of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite can be divided into three peaks. As presented in Fig. 2g, the corresponding fitting peaks at 287.86 eV, 286.17 eV and 284.76 eV can be attributed to the CN, CO and graphitic (CC and CC), respectively [15,21,29]. The XPS results further confirmed that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and textural property of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic BiVO4, Cu2O, BiVO4/Cu2O and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite were characterized by FESEM to explore the morphology. In Fig. 3 a and e, the pure BiVO4 presents plate-like structure which surface is smooth. As shown in Fig. 3b and f, the Cu2O displays sphere-like structure and stacked together. The particles average exhibit the diameter about 300–600 nm. The SEM images of BiVO4/Cu2O are presented in Fig. 3c and g, revealing that the sphere-like structures Cu2O loaded on the BiVO4 plates. Similarly, both Cu2O and BiVO4 plates can be found in BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite (Fig. 3d and h). However, the N-CQDs are difficult to be characterized by FESEM due to its small size (<10 nm) [29].

To further investigate the detailed microstructure and morphology of the pure BiVO4, Cu2O, N-CQDs and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are provided in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, BiVO4 displays plate-like shape with a smooth surface. The TEM image of Cu2O in Fig. 4b indicates that Cu2O is sphere-like shape and the average diameter of Cu2O is about 300–600 nm. The TEM images of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite (Fig. 4c and d) confirm that the sphere-like Cu2O uniformly distributed on the surface of the BiVO4. As presented in Fig. 4e, the N-CQDs hold a spherical-like shape with the diameter of 5 nm. HRTEM image of the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite is shown in Fig. 4g and the region of HRTEM is marked in Fig. 4f. The lattice spaces of 0.24, 0.214 and 0.33 nm can be assigned to (0 2 0) crystallographic planes of the monoclinic BiVO4, (2 0 0) crystal plane of Cu2O and (0 0 2) crystal plane of N-CQDs, respectively. The results demonstrate that BiVO4, Cu2O and N-CQDs are intimately contacted would promote to charge transfer and photocatalytic activity. To investigate the element types and the distribution of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite, the HAADF-STEM images and the corresponding EDS mapping images are provided in Fig. 4h–n. It is clear that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite contains Bi, V, O, Cu, N and C elements. Furthermore, the good distribution of these elements predicates the Cu2O and N-CQDs were successfully composited with BiVO4.

Photoluminescence (PL) technique has been applied to study the separation and recombination processes of the photoexcited electrons and holes [30,31]. Generally, the weaker PL intensity implies that the higher separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Fig. 5 shows the PL spectra of the pure BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites. All samples exhibited strong emission peaks at around 547 nm. The pure BiVO4 possessed a relatively high recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The PL intensity was decreased after BiVO4 combined with N-CQDs and Cu2O, respectively. And the PL intensity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was lower than that of N-CQDs/BiVO4 and Cu2O/BiVO4 composites. The result suggests that the separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs was improved after the combination of BiVO4, N-CQDs and Cu2O, which is essential to the photocatalytic process.

The relative band gap of the photocatalysts are calculated by the formula:
(1)
α h υ = Φ
h υ -
E g
n / 2
where α, h, υ, Φ and Eg are absorption coefficient, planck constant (Φ = 1), light frequency, proportionality constant and band gap energy, respectively. The value of n is decided by the characteristics of the semiconductor (n = 1 for direct band semiconductor and n = 2 for indirect band semiconductor). The relative band gap energy of the photocatalysts can be estimated from the intercept of the tangent to the plot of (αhυ) 2 radiation energy. As presented in Fig. 6b, the band gap energies of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Cu2O/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O are 2.38, 2.36, 2.28 and 2.11 eV, respectively. Hence, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite holds the narrowest band gap energy among the as-prepared photocatalysts, which could utilize the solar energy efficiently and lead to an enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The photoelectrochemical properties characteristics of the as-prepared photocatalysts were measured to further study separation and recombination processes of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [32,35]. Therefore, the transient photocurrent response (i-t) and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) were performed. As presented in Fig. 7 a, all as-prepared photocatalysts exhibited transient photocurrent under visible light irradiation, while the photocurrent became weak quickly in the dark condition. Notably, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite exhibits the highest transient photocurrent intensity, revealing that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite possesses an efficient electron-hole pairs separation efficiency. The results of EIS analysis are shown in Fig. 7b. In general, the semicircle diameter of EIS Nyquist plot refers to the charge-transfer resistance. And the smaller semicircle diameter of EIS Nyquist plot means that the lower charge-transfer resistance. Notably, of all the synthetic samples, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite presents the smallest semicircle diameter which indicating that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite holds the highest charge carrier transfer efficiency.

The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) over the BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 composites, Cu2O/BiVO4 composites and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composites were evaluated under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performances of the photocatalysts with different contents of N-CQDs and Cu2O were studied. The results are presented in Fig. 8 . The results of TC photodegradation under visible light irradiation over BiVO4/N-CQDs with the content of N-CQDs (1, 3, 5, 7 mL) are shown in Fig. 8a. For the pure BiVO4, the photodegradation efficiency of TC is only 50.9% after 60 min visible light irradiation. After the N-CQDs anchored on the surface of BiVO4, the photocatalytic performance had been obviously improved. However, BiVO4/N-CQDs composites showed a decreased degradation efficiency with the increase of N-CQDs, and BiVO4/N-CQDs-1 showed the highest photodegradation efficiency (75.7%). This phenomenon could be explained by two reasons. Firstly, an appropriate amount of N-CQDs that could promote the separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs while the excessive content of N-CQDs may cover the active sites of BiVO4. Secondly, the N-CQDs may act as a recombination center of electrons and holes that is harmful to the photocatalytic activities.

The surface defects sites on the N-CQDs is beneficial improve the photocatalytic performance [21,29]. While the photoexcited electrons of N-CQDs/BiVO4 are transfer to a lower energy level, which is adverse to the enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability. Hence, the construction of Z-scheme system is vital to further improve the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4/N-CQDs. Under the optimum condition, the Cu2O was composited with BiVO4/N-CQDs-1. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, the photodegradation efficiency of TC was decreased with the mass ratio of Cu2O increased to 15%. While the photodegradation efficiency of TC showed a slight increasing when the mass ratio of Cu2O increased to 20%. The BiVO4/N-CQDs-1/Cu2O-5% composite showed the highest photodegradation efficiency (99.1%). The appropriate amount of Cu2O and N-CQDs/BiVO4 showed a synergetic effect in the process of photodegradation.

The summary of the photocatalytic degradation of TC based on different photocatalysts helps to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance. As illustrated in Fig. 8c and d, BiVO4/N-CQDs-1/Cu2O-5% shows the highest photodegradation efficiency with 99.1% in 60 min. And the degradation efficiency can be arranged in the order BiVO4 < BiVO4/N-CQDs-1 < BiVO4/Cu2O-5% < BiVO4/N-CQDs-1/Cu2O-5%. Compared with the pure BiVO4, BiVO4/N-CQDs-1, BiVO4/Cu2O-5% and BiVO4/N-CQDs-1/Cu2O-5% showed an enhanced photodegradation efficiency, implying that synergistic effect existed among N-CQDs, Cu2O and BiVO4.

To evaluate the stability of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite, recycle experiments for the photodegradation of TC under the same conditions were conducted. As displayed in Fig. 9 a, after five recycles, the degradation efficiency of TC by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite still maintained at 96.6%, implying that the photocatalyst possesses high stability. Furthermore, the changes in crystalline phase of photocatalyst was further confirmed by X-ray diffraction. The result is presented in Fig. 9b, no obvious changes in crystalline phase of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was observed after five recycles. Consequently, all of above results implying that BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite holds remarkable stability and recyclability.

In order to study the reaction mechanism of photocatalytic process, the active species trapping experiments have been carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the photodegradation efficiency of TC over BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite was severely inhibited after the 1, 4-benzoquinone (BQ) was added, demonstrating that
O2 − is the major radical involved reaction process. Similarly, isopropanol (IPA) and triethanolamine (TEA) posed a negative effect on the photodegradation efficiency, indicating both
OH and h+ involved in the photodegradation process. Consequently, all the active species (
O2 −,
OH and h+) are produced under visible light irradiation and involved in the photodegradation of TC process.

To further study the mechanism of photodegradation process by the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite, the ESR measurements with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were conducted. And the results are presented in Fig. 11. As shown in Fig. 11 a, no signals for DMPO-
O2− were found under the dark condition. While strong signals of DMPO-
O2− species under visible light irradiation for 5 and 10 min revealing that the photoexcited electrons from BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite could reduce O2 to
O2− in solution under the visible light irradiation. Similarly, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH were appeared under visible light irradiation for 5 and 10 min, no signals can be found under the dark (Fig. 11b). Furthermore, both the intensity of DMPO-
O2− signals and DMPO-
OH signals all became strong from 5 min to 10 min. Consequently, h+ and
O2− all involved in the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O photodegradation process which leading to the enormous enhanced photocatalytic performance.

On the basis of the aforementioned experimental results and analyses, a Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism of photodegradation over BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 12 . Generally, the BiVO4 and Cu2O forms a p-n heterojunction, which is beneficial to the separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. While, the active species would not produce in p-n heterojunction due to the reduction reaction/oxidation reaction take place on a semiconductor with lower reduction potential/lower oxidation potential [11,36]. In this study, a Z-scheme system was constructed due to both
O2− and
OH are detected by ESR spin-trap technique and trapping experiments. According to the previous literatures, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) position of BiVO4 were calculated to be +0.46 eV and +2.86 eV [37]. And for Cu2O, they were −1.4 eV and 1.8 eV, respectively [10,26]. Under the visible light, the electrons on the CB of BiVO4 were produced and promptly captured by holes (h+) of N-CQDs [29,38–40]. Part of the photoexcited electrons reduced O2 to generate
O2 − due to a large number of defect sites on the surface of N-CQDs [15,29]. And the h+ remained on N-CQDs also participated in the photocatalytic reaction. Simultaneously, photoexcited h+ on the VB of BiVO4 could directly oxidize the TC, even react with H2O to produce
OH active species due to the fact that VB of BiVO4 is more positive enough than the standard redox potential of OH−/
OH (2.40 eV vs. NHE) [41]. Therefore, the N-CQDs provide a potential driving force to construct an internal Z-scheme charge transfer system. While other part of the photoexcited electrons recombination with the h+ on the VB of Cu2O. Subsequently, photoexcited electrons excited to the CB of Cu2O. Thus,
O2 − was produced from O2 in water due to the CB (−1.4 eV) of Cu2O is negative enough for reducing O2 to
O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE) [42]. It should be noted that the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs is overwhelming improved and the strong redox ability is maintained at the same time in the Z-scheme system. Moreover, the molecular oxygen activation of the composite greatly enhanced by contacting N-CQDs and the construction of Z-scheme system. Consequently,
O2 −,
OH and h+ participated in the photocatalytic reaction process and lead to an excellent photocatalytic performance. In addition, the Cu2O provided multiple reflection of visible light and the N-CQDs act as a photosensitizer all contributed to the enhanced visible light absorption, which is beneficial to photodegradation activity.

In conclusion, in this study, a novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) promoted Z-scheme BiVO/N-CQDs/Cu2O system with enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability was successfully constructed. The Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Cu2O composite exhibited an excellent photocatalytic performance, presenting 99.1% tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal efficiency under visible light irradiation in 60 min. The N-CQDs provide a potential driving force to reestablish the local electric field and lead to the construction of the Z-scheme BiVO/N-CQDs/Cu2O system. Thus, the efficient separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs was achieved. In addition,
O2 −,
OH and h+ were involved in the photocatalytic reaction process, which was confirmed by ESR measurements and trapping experiments. Noteworthily, the molecular oxygen activation of the composite greatly enhanced by contacting N-CQDs and the construction of Z-scheme system. Moreover, the visible light absorption capability was obviously improved due to the fact that Cu2O provided multiple reflection of visible light and the N-CQDs act as the photosensitizer. This research offers a new perspective of the photocatalytic mechanism based on nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and inspires the potential of design the highly efficient Z-scheme system for application in wastewater remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the Key Projects of National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 51739004, 71431006), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21776066) and Hunan Provincial Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (CX2017B096).
